summary: On a runtime-opaque bug, does strong-prompted rdbg come in cheaper than a control read loop?

summary.roi_verdict: No — the debugger still did NOT pay off, even on this harder runtime-opaque bug. Strong-prompted rdbg (adoption 4/5) averaged 277,938 tokens vs control's 152,991 — 1.82x more expensive — and 52.5s vs 25.7s wall (2.04x slower). Both conditions passed 5/5, so the extra cost bought ZERO reliability gain. The premise that a value-opaque bug (swapped operands where the wrong result doesn't localize the fault) would finally justify the debugger did not hold: the plain read loop matched rdbg's perfect pass rate at ~half the tokens and half the wall time. Nuance vs the easy bug: the token penalty did shrink from ~2.85x to 1.82x — the harder bug narrowed the gap, consistent with rdbg being relatively more useful when the output doesn't point at the bug — but it is still net-negative (more cost, no benefit). Corroborating signal within the strong arm: the one run that skipped rdbg (idx4: 149k tokens, 29.2s) was the cheapest strong run and matched control, while the four rdbg runs averaged 310k tokens — i.e., invoking rdbg itself roughly doubled cost with no pass-rate upside (control already at 100%). Verdict: on a 5/5-vs-5/5 pass tie, cheaper wins; the read loop is the better ROI, and forceful rdbg mandating remains unjustified here.

workflowProgress.label: run:strong-0

Run EXACTLY this one command and wait for it to finish (it launches a headless Claude Code session on a debugging task, ~1-8 min) — it prints a single JSON object on its last stdout line. Return that JSON's fields verbatim; run nothing else.

workflowProgress.promptPreview:
10 runs of Claude Code (Opus, medium effort) fixing the SAME failing test in a runtime-opaque RPN calculator (a swapped operand order on non-commutative ops — the wrong final value does not point at the bug). 'strong' = forceful CLAUDE.md mandating rdbg; 'control' = neutral note. rdbg was available in both.
